notes: Stored URL resolves to 'John Taylor (jazz)', British jazz pianist (1942-2015) -- our entity. All checkable claims confirmed: birth 1942, death 2015, piano, British; trio Azimuth (formed 1977 with Norma Winstone and Kenny Wheeler, several ECM recordings); extensive Kenny Wheeler collaboration. 'Central to the ECM aesthetic' and 'finest European jazz' are editorial, consistent with the ECM work in evidence.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead (genre list, 'deliberately resists category' and Rolling Stone quotes all match exactly). Born September 2, 1953 confirmed; deathYear null consistent with '(born 1953)' short description; 'composer' confirmed.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead and fully confirmed: b. July 25 1907, d. May 11 1970, alto sax with Ellington, refused soprano after 1940, Benny Carter comparison. Sole unchecked item: instruments field lists 'clarinet', which the evidence never attributes to Hodges (his early instruments were drums and piano, then soprano sax). Unsupported, not contradicted -- absence is not contradiction. Repair-writer should source or drop the clarinet entry; everything else is clean.

Bio is near-verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: b. 1986 (Nov 11) confirmed; collaborator list is an exact subset of the wiki list (wiki adds Doja Cat); Stay Human as Late Show house band 2015 to 2022 confirmed verbatim. Instruments 'composer' (Soul score, film scores, symphony) and 'bandleader' confirmed. Minor: ours says 'singer...composer' where the wiki lead says 'musician...record producer'; 'singer' is not explicitly stated in the extract but is a profession descriptor, not contradicted.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: b. July 24 1953; trumpet player, conductor, composer, educator; Dizzy Gillespie mentorship 'along with pianist Stan Kenton and trumpeter Bill Catalano' matches the wiki text exactly. Instruments [trumpet, composer] confirmed.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead. Confirmed: b. December 25 1987; 2000 Grammy Awards performance at 12; Stanford Jazz Workshop faculty at 15; debut Sounding Point (EmArcy, 2009); Blue Note signing 2021; all five listed Blue Note releases incl. Scenes from Above (2026) appear in both the lead and the discography. Guitar confirmed.

Confirmed: b. Jan 24 1938, d. April 2 1995; 'performed mainly on alto saxophone, less often on soprano and tenor saxophones and flute' supports alto/flute/composer; co-founded the Black Artists' Group in St. Louis (verbatim 'co-founded'); World Saxophone Quartet founding (wiki: 'founder of the World Saxophone Quartet, a group he formed in 1976' -- ours' 'co-founded' is compatible, not contradicted); Dogon A.D. in discography (Mbari, 1972). 'One of the great avant-garde jazz recordings', 'theatrical', 'uncompromising' are editorial.

Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: b. Feb 18 1981; raised in Los Angeles; sax on Kendrick Lamar's To Pimp a Butterfly and on Flying Lotus's You're Dead!; The Epic released May 2015 as his debut solo recording; West Coast Get Down founding member supports the 'community of like-minded players' framing. Unchecked substantive claims: The Epic being a 'triple album' (format not stated in evidence); 'jazz-musician father' (father never mentioned); 'tenor' saxophone specifically (evidence says only 'saxophonist'); Coltrane/Pharoah Sanders lineage is a stylistic claim not in evidence.

Right entity, no contradictions. Confirmed: b. May 8 1945; The Koln Concert (1975) 'best-selling piano recording in history' (ours' wider 'best-selling solo jazz and piano album' is not contradicted); the 1970s 'American quartet' (Haden/Motian/Redman) named in evidence; 2018 stroke ending performances; jazz+classical span. Unchecked substantive claims: 'Standards Trio with Gary Peacock and Jack DeJohnette' -- the extract truncates in the early 1970s and Gary Peacock never appears in it; 'demanding concert requirements and onstage vocalizations' not in evidence.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead (ours says 'saxophonist, and clarinetist' where wiki lead says 'reedist'; wiki then specifies 'plays tenor saxophone, clarinet, bass clarinet, and baritone saxophone', confirming both instruments). b. September 22 1964, Chicago scene since the 1990s, 1999 MacArthur Fellowship all confirmed.

Confirmed: b. July 31 1931 in Detroit; work on Prestige, Blue Note and other top labels; Midnight Blue (1963, with Stanley Turrentine) cited by critics as a highlight; Ellington devotion ('Ellingtonia' UCLA course, Ellington called him his favorite guitarist, recorded Ellington tributes); Director of Jazz Studies at UCLA since 1996. 'Quintessential Blue Note guitarist', 'one of the most recorded guitarists in jazz history' and 'hundreds of sessions' are editorial-scale characterizations consistent with the evidence's 'prolific recording career' and studio-musician demand.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: Kenneth Clarke Spearman, 'Klook', bebop drumming innovator, ride-cymbal timekeeping, bass-drum irregular accents. b. January 9 1914, d. January 26 1985, drums all confirmed.
Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead, including the Gary Giddins 'virtually synonymous with underrated' quote. b. August 30 1924, d. December 5 1972, trumpet confirmed.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead and fully confirmed: b. October 9 1960; Duke Ellington Orchestra (under Mercer Ellington, 1978); Miles Davis band; solo career since 1985; 'primary instruments are alto and soprano saxophone and flute'. Sole unchecked item: instruments field also lists 'piano', which the evidence never attributes to Garrett. Unsupported, not contradicted. Repair-writer should source or drop the piano entry; everything else is clean.

Confirmed: b. January 14 1930 (Toronto), d. September 18 2014; trumpet and flugelhorn; 'Canadian-British' consistent with 'Canadian... based in the U.K. from the 1950s onwards'. ECM albums with Keith Jarrett (Gnu High, 1975 -- 'one of the few albums to feature Keith Jarrett as a sideman') and with Dave Holland (Angel Song, ECM 1997, with Holland on bass) both confirmed. 'Masterpieces' and 'European chamber jazz aesthetic' are editorial; the chamber-jazz framing is supported by the Azimuth description.

Confirmed: cornet player and bandleader; pioneering use of mutes; 'the mentor and teacher of Louis Armstrong' (ours' 'most important mentor' superlative is editorial but directly supported by Armstrong's own quote); Creole Jazz Band's 1923 recordings for Gennett/Okeh/Paramount/Columbia. Dates 1881-1938 match the short description; wiki notes 1881 is self-claimed and debated (some sources say 1884/85) -- our 1881 follows the page's headline dates. Nuance: ours says 'from New Orleans'; evidence: born in Aben, Louisiana (near Donaldsonville), 'moved to New Orleans in his youth' -- loose phrasing, not contradicted, but a precision-minded repair could say 'Louisiana-born, raised in New Orleans'.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: guitarist, composer, bandleader, producer, educator, keyboardist, record label owner. b. October 28 1970 confirmed. Instruments [guitar, piano] supported: piano lessons at nine and 'keyboardist' in the lead.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia article (it even reproduces the source's grammar slip 'founded a jazz trio and performing in local clubs'). Confirmed: b. December 12 1977; Norwegian, lives in New York; skateboarding ambition; guitar at 13; Berklee scholarship and Wally's Cafe performances. Guitar confirmed.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: organist and occasional pianist, Jimmy Smith soul-jazz influence, later experimental/modal Hammond B-3 approach. b. October 7 1940, d. March 30 1978, organ confirmed. Stored URL is the disambiguated 'Larry Young (musician)' page about our organist.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia article: Swedish jazz pianist and composer b. 1951; Orebro upbringing, boredom at community music school, relative's Miles Davis/Ben Webster/Mose Allison records, 1960s organ interest -- all confirmed word-for-word. Stored URL is the disambiguated 'Lars Jansson (composer)' page and it is about our pianist.
One-sentence bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead. b. October 13 1927, d. April 15 2020, alto saxophone confirmed.

Right entity, no provable contradictions. Confirmed: b. July 10 1938, d. February 19 1972; 'joined Dizzy Gillespie's big band at 18' (verbatim); The Sidewinder title track cracked the pop chart in 1964; Search for the New Land (1964); heroin problems (Blakey said to have introduced him); shot by Helen Moore at Slugs' Saloon and bled to death. Unchecked: 'recording his first Blue Note album at 19' -- evidence says 'Morgan began recording for Blue Note in 1956' (he turned 18 July 1956), which suggests 18, but the same page also says he 'started to record as leader in the late '50s' and never dates the first album, so the 19 claim is neither confirmable nor verbatim-contradicted; flag for repair review. Ours' 'common-law wife' vs evidence's 'live-in girlfriend' is compatible phrasing; 'murder' is consistent with her shooting him, his death, and her arrest/imprisonment.
One-sentence bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead: pianist, composer, arranger, and teacher of jazz improvisation. b. March 19 1919, d. November 18 1978, piano confirmed.

The stored URL (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leon_Thomas_(musician)) returned page: null -- no evidence document survived, nothing checkable. All claims pending verification: 1937-1999 dates, vocals, Pygmy-inspired yodeling technique, work with Pharoah Sanders on 'Karma'. Needs a re-fetch of the page (the null may be a fetch failure or a moved/disambiguated title) before any verdict on content; the stored link itself also remains unverified.

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: was an American jazz tenor saxophonist and occasional clarinetist

notes: Right entity; the bio itself is verbatim from the Wikipedia lead and fully confirmed (b. Aug 27 1909, d. March 15 1959, 'Pres'/'Prez', tenor saxophonist and occasional clarinetist). The error is in the structured instruments field: it lists ONLY 'clarinet', omitting tenor saxophone -- the instrument the evidence (and our own bio text) identifies him by; short description is 'American jazz saxophonist (1909-1959)' and the lead calls him 'President of the tenor saxophone'. Clarinet is not false as a secondary instrument, but as the sole instruments entry it misidentifies his instrument to users. Looks like an extraction slip that kept 'occasional clarinetist' and dropped 'tenor saxophonist'. Repair: instruments should be ['tenor saxophone', 'clarinet'] per the evidence.
